medications.drug_name: additional oral gliclazide/metformin dose to lower blood sugar at the facility
medications.indication_rationale: Oral hypoglycaemics are inappropriate and unsafe in this setting; glucose correction in HHS must occur via carefully monitored IV fluids (and often IV insulin) in hospital.
contraindications_in_this_case: Suspected hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state with profound dehydration

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Position the patient supine with legs elevated, monitor his airway and consciousness continuously, and do not attempt oral rehydration given his confusion and drowsiness.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Supports blood pressure while awaiting transport; oral fluids risk aspiration given his reduced consciousness.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a serious diabetic emergency called hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state — his blood sugar and dehydration have built up gradually but are now very dangerous, even without the fast breathing sometimes seen in other diabetic emergencies.
Call 000 for an ambulance right now — he needs careful intravenous fluids in hospital, given slowly and monitored closely.
Don't give him any more diabetes tablets or try to give him fluids by mouth given how drowsy and confused he is.
Mention the possible urine infection to the ambulance and hospital team, as this may have triggered this crisis and will need treatment too.

follow_up_plan.interval: Immediate emergency transport and hospital admission for IV fluid resuscitation
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Serum osmolality and glucose trend, identification/treatment of the precipitating infection, and post-discharge diabetes and cognitive/functional review
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Not applicable — already an emergency requiring immediate transport

must_include_items: Explicit recognition that this is HHS despite the absence of Kussmaul breathing/ketotic breath
Advice against oral rehydration or further oral diabetes medication
Mention of the suspected UTI as a likely precipitant needing treatment
errors_of_omission: No mention of the suspected precipitating infection
errors_of_commission: Dismissing this as 'just a UTI' or less urgent than DKA because ketotic features are absent
Recommending oral fluids or additional oral diabetes medication
Recommending only routine facility GP review the next day
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Failing to recognise HHS as an equally or more life-threatening emergency than DKA, simply because classic ketoacidosis features are absent, is a critical safety failure.
